title: Vanilla slice
subtitle: Rough puff pastry, vanilla crème pâtissière, fondant icing
excerpt: Two layers of baked rough puff pastry sandwiching a thick set crème pâtissière, finished with white fondant icing. The custard must be firm enough to hold a clean edge when sliced. A British bakery staple, also called a custard slice.
sourceNotes: The vanilla or custard slice in its British form is a simplified version of the French mille-feuille, adapted for commercial bakery production and home baking in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. British versions typically use a thick, set crème pâtissière rather than the whipped cream versions seen in Australian and New Zealand adaptations. The rough puff base is the standard home-cook substitute for full laminated pastry, documented in British cookery writing from the mid-twentieth century.

content.text: The key to a clean-slicing vanilla slice is a very firm crème pâtissière. Increase the cornflour beyond what you would use for a tart filling; it needs to hold a straight edge under the weight of the pastry.
bakes unevenly because the butter is not uniformly distributed; weighting it under a second tray while it bakes keeps the sheets flat. The
sets firm enough to score with a knife before slicing, score through the icing before cutting all the way down to avoid cracking the surface.

attrs.prepNote: more than a standard tart cream, for a firmer set

content.text: Add the cold butter cubes to the flour and salt. Rub briefly so the butter is coated in flour but the cubes are still intact, do not rub to breadcrumbs. Add the cold water and mix until the dough comes together in a shaggy mass with visible butter chunks. On a floured surface, roll into a long rectangle and fold into thirds. Rotate 90 degrees, roll and fold again. Repeat for 4 folds total. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll into two rectangles to fit a 30 × 20 cm tin, trimmed to the same size. Bake at 200°C (180°C fan) for 20 to 22 minutes weighted under a second tray until deep amber and crisp. Cool completely on a rack.

Heat the milk with the vanilla pod to just below a simmer. Whisk the egg yolks, sugar, and cornflour in a bowl until smooth. Pour a third of the hot milk into the egg mixture while whisking, then return to the pan. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until the cream boils and thickens to a very stiff consistency, 4 to 5 minutes. Remove from heat and beat in the butter. Press film directly onto the surface and refrigerate until cold and set.

Line a 30 × 20 cm tin with baking paper, overhanging on two sides. Place one pastry sheet in the base. Beat the cold crème pâtissière to smooth it, then spread evenly over the pastry to a consistent depth of about 2 cm. Place the second pastry sheet on top and press gently. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until set firm. Beat the icing sugar with water to a thick, spreadable consistency. Remove the slab from the tin using the paper, place on a board, and spread the fondant over the top pastry. Leave to set for 20 minutes. Score the icing with a sharp knife into 9 portions, then cut through cleanly.
